title: Potato Gnocchi with Butter and Herbs

ingredients.name: starchy potatoes (e.g. Russet or Kufri), boiled and peeled
maida (all-purpose flour), plus extra for dusting

subs.name: rice flour (50%) + maida (50%) for a lighter, chewier gnocchi
subs.notes: reduces gluten; gives a slightly smoother surface

subs.name: omit entirely — the recipe works without it
subs.notes: eggless gnocchi are slightly more delicate; handle them gently when cutting

subs.name: olive oil or a vegan spread
subs.notes: olive oil gives a lighter, fruitier finish; great with just basil and pepper

ingredients.name: Parmesan or Pecorino, grated, to finish

subs.name: a sprinkle of nutritional yeast for a vegan topping
subs.notes: omit butter and use olive oil for a fully plant-based version

steps.text: Boil the potatoes whole in their skins until completely tender — a skewer should pass through with no resistance, about 20–25 minutes. Drain, and while still hot, peel and pass them through a ricer or mash thoroughly until completely lump-free. Spread on a tray to let steam escape for 5 minutes — dry potatoes absorb less flour and make lighter gnocchi.

Transfer the cooled mashed potato to a clean work surface. Make a well, add the salt and flour (and the egg yolk if using). Work the mixture together gently with a dough scraper or your hands just until it comes together into a soft, slightly sticky dough. Do not overwork — overworked gnocchi become dense and rubbery.

Divide the dough into 6 portions. Roll each into a rope about 1.5 cm thick on a lightly floured surface. Cut each rope into 2 cm pillows. If you wish, roll each pillow gently over the back of a fork to create ridges that hold the sauce.

Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil. Drop in the gnocchi in batches — do not crowd the pot. They are ready when they float to the surface and then cook for a further 30–45 seconds. Fish out with a slotted spoon and set aside.

In a wide frying pan, melt the butter over medium-high heat until it foams and turns pale golden with a nutty fragrance — browned butter, about 2–3 minutes. Add the cooked gnocchi and toss to coat. Add the basil, season with black pepper.
Plate immediately into warm bowls. Top with freshly grated Parmesan and serve without delay — gnocchi lose their texture as they sit.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: maida → rice flour (50%) + maida (50%) for a lighter, chewier gnocchi (reduces gluten; gives a slightly smoother surface); cheese → a sprinkle of nutritional yeast for a vegan topping (omit butter and use olive oil for a fully plant-based version); butter → olive oil or a vegan spread (olive oil gives a lighter, fruitier finish; great with just basil and pepper); egg → omit entirely — the recipe works without it (eggless gnocchi are slightly more delicate; handle them gently when cutting)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Indian potatoes — particularly the high-starch white Kufri or Jyoti varieties — make excellent gnocchi dough. Boil and rice or mash them very dry for the best texture. Amul butter is perfect for the browned-butter finish. If no ricer is available, press the potatoes through a steel sieve with the back of a spoon. The gnocchi can also be pan-fried in butter in a kadai after boiling for extra golden edges.
